text: Chapter 16 teaches the fundamental harmonies built from scales, establishing that every scale contains inherent diatonic chords derived through the next-chord-up construction — moving each tone in a chord up to the next scale tone to build the sequence. The master's characteristic moves in this section include voicing rules for practical application: using open triads with root-position bass at phrase endings, doubling triad tones (root, 3rd, or 5th) to create four-note chords, and omitting the root or 5th when reducing seventh chords to triad form for harmonization. Load-bearing terminology includes open voicing (any arrangement other than closed), diatonic seventh-chord sequences (I, ii–m7, iii–m7, IV, V7, vi–m7, vii°), and the critical insight that vii° (diminished triad) functions as a rootless V7 — a substitution principle for modulation. The chapter frames these techniques in the context of practical applications like Christmas carol harmonization and key transitions, where V7 or V always sets up modulation to a new key.
